g.data-narration: Welcome to Dynamic Array Stack - solving the fixed-size stack limitations with runtime resizing.
Fixed-size stacks have key problems: overflow when full, memory waste when mostly empty, and runtime inflexibility.
The solution: use malloc to allocate initial array, then realloc to double capacity when full - adapt to actual needs!
The DynamicStack struct has three fields: data pointer, top index, and capacity for tracking array size.
Push operation checks if full, doubles capacity with realloc if needed, then inserts the new element at top index.
Amortized O(1) means average cost per push is constant. While resizing costs O(n), it happens rarely enough to average out.
g.text: Although one resize costs O(n), average over many pushes is O(1)

g.data-narration: Pop removes the top element and optionally shrinks the array when usage drops below 25% of capacity to save memory.
Thrashing occurs when shrink threshold is too aggressive - alternating push/pop causes endless resize cycles, wasting CPU.
g.text: Halving at 1/2 causes endless resize cycles at boundary
Now shrinking only happens at 25% usage

g.data-narration: The resize function uses realloc to change array size. Always save to temp first to avoid memory leaks on failure!
g.text: If realloc fails, temp is NULL

g.data-narration: Complete C implementation includes init, push, pop, peek, display, size, and destroy functions for full stack lifecycle.
Watch how the stack grows: starts at capacity 4, doubles at push 5 to 8, doubles again at push 9 to 16.
Compare fixed vs dynamic stacks: fixed is simple O(1) but wastes memory; dynamic adapts but needs careful memory management.
Three key design choices: initial capacity (small power of 2), growth factor (2 for O(1) amortized), shrink threshold (1/4).
In summary: dynamic stacks use realloc to resize, achieve amortized O(1) push, and require proper destroy to free memory.
Exam tips: Remember struct fields, explain amortized analysis, show temp pointer for realloc, and always include destroy function.
g.text: 1. Show struct with data, top, capacity fields 2. Explain amortized O(1) proof 3. Use temp for realloc 4. Include destroy function 5. Know thrashing vs halve-at-quarter
